He was not able to give us the particulars of the story, but next day, sitting on the Mission portico overlooking the gardens, the yellow, weather-beaten walls speaking of the long-ago Spanish days, we heard the pathetic tale from one who had been almost an actor in it, old
when she was a slip of a girl attending
. "It is more than sixty years ago now, yet I remember
as if it were last week. She was the gayest and brightest, and with all her willfullness,
the best-loved of Padre Juan's pupils
. When she grew up and fell in love with
that she owed her father's reluctant consent to her marriage.
was the best vaquero within a hundred miles of

, and in the processions on fiestas and the "Sixteenth" he was the finest rider as well as the handsomest man among the caballeros. But although a really superior fellow of his class, he was hardly a match for
, a blue-blooded Spaniard, who had been exiled from
after one of the political upheavals, felt bitter about the marriage; for although poor, he was proud as
"As things came out, it was a great pity that the marriage ever took place; but neither
nor any one else could foresee that after ten years of exile
was to be recalled to his Mexican honors, and that
whom he could tolerate in poverty would be unendurable in prosperity. But so God willed it.
had to make the hard choice between

go from her forever. It caused her the deepest pain she had ever known, for although he had never been quite the same to her since the marriage, she loved him passionately. Pobrecita, it was only the beginning of her troubles. Bravely she choked her sobs, and smiled with the dull ache in her breast, until her mind was engrossed with other cares.
"A month or two after the birth of
was given charge of a band of Indians, who were to slaughter the cattle on
, with periods of fertility and barrenness. For a few years it may be richly covered with alfilaria, and considering its size, is the best of

for stock-raising. Then, without any apparent cause, the sea will pile up on the sand on the beach, and the wind began to drive it inland, covering the rich pasture, and making a desert of almost the whole island. Such a period had come after several years of profitable stock-raising, and the cattle were to be killed for the hides and tallow. As the work would take two or three months,
"Just before they left, I was ordered to the
, and only when I returned five years later, did I hear the sad story of Pedro's death and Mercedes' disappearance.
, it appeared, having completed his work, and seen to the shipment of the hides and tallow on
one of the big American vessels
that in those days came around
to buy these products, had come to
in a small boat to make arrangements for bringing off his men and their families.
early in August, found it in wild excitement ever the threatened American invasion. More history had been made in the few months of his absence on
than in all his life in
before. The Bear Flag rebellion had taken place, and war had now broken out between
, the governor, had impressed all able-bodied men into a force to resist attack; and
, nothing loath, since he knew all would be well on
for a few weeks, was ordered to join the force. A few days later
and demanded its surrender. When this was refused, a body of sailors landed, and in the fighting that followed several men were killed. Poor Pedro's daring made him one of the first victims.
the boat for which he had come
was left behind. It was a cold, damp evening when
reached the landing place, and clouds banking up on the horizon gave promise of an early storm.
, knowing that if a storm should come, it might be a week before he could land
, determined to put back the same night, feeling secure that by sailing at once he could land before the storm should break.
had been ailing, and rather than expose
to the cold and rain of the long night voyage, she said she would wait until
should return for the belongings of the camp.
wished to remain with her, but
was expected to return in a few days; and in the confusion of the hurried embarkation, the cruelty of leaving her alone with the heart-breaking news
had brought, was little noticed by
"The troublous times caused the elapse of two weeks before
, went with it; but on reaching
, he could find no trace of
. The crew spent two days in gathering up the effects of the camp, and

searched high and low without avail. In the house she had occupied moldy fragments of food, rusted cooking utensils and the dust gave evidence that she had been absent many days. Knowing that boats from
, the sailors concluded she had been taken to
by one of them; and with this hope

. It remained one of the dark mysteries of the sea. Some thought a smuggling vessel might have picked her up and carried her to a far distant land. Smugglers were not uncommon, although
is farther out than they usually thought it necessary to stay. The favorite theory among the people of
, brooding over her father's relentlessness, Pedro's death and her child's illness, became unbalanced and destroyed both
"Padre Juan's declining years were clouded and his life shortened by the tragic fate of
. He spent much of his time in making inquiries among such travelers and seafaring men as came to
. Especially he cultivated the smugglers and their friends. As his hopes died away, he sunk into apathy, hardly noticing anything about him, and rousing himself only to ask
whether he had been to blame for his part in bringing about Mercedes' marriage. He died within two years, and was buried at his request in the mound yonder overlooking the sea.
"Years and years passed away. In the many alterations brought by the American, the discovery of gold, the great influx of people and the changes in the mode of life,
were almost forgotten, and the story of
became a faint memory. So far as is known
was never visited again until the spring of 1864 a party from
explored the lesser islands in quest of game.

, going a mile or so into the interior, found a rude hut built against a huge rock overlooking a little valley. In front of the hut a fireplace contained the ashed of a recent fire, and hanging inside was what appeared to be the dress of a woman. Before leaving

went to see the place. They concluded it must have been the camp of hunters that had been there but a short time before, and they wondered at the smallness of the hut, its air of permanency, and more than all, at the old dress.
and told what they had seen, the story of
was revived. A painful interest stirred the community. People that remembered
, her happy childhood, sorrowful wedded life and strange disappearance looked at one another with the question in their eyes they could hardly put into words.
was quickly made up to go to
and search it from end to end. I was asked to be one of the party, because, of the few now left of those who had known and loved her in the happy days of her youth, I seemed to be the nearest.

"Never can I forget the morning we found her. One of those beautiful spring mornings when the sea is laughing, the sky cloudless, the poppies and lilies gathered in tens of thousands to dance their joy, as if earth had no such thing as sorrow. The sands had retreated from
, and once again it was a garden of loveliness. It seemed a mockery that this should be the scene of such a story."
paused, gazing for some moments into vacancy, his eyes filling with tears. Then, before he went on, he spent some time in counting the beads of his rosary.
the gay and handsome Spanish girl
we knew eighteen years before was
, her once bright faculties heavy and dull. We found her within a mile of the western end of

and I came suddenly upon her seated in a camp formed by a low wall of rocks. She was weaving willow shoots together, making a kind of basket. Her dress was of feathers held in a network of sinew cords. Under a roof of boughs in a corner of the enclosure hung the dress of faded cloth, which the hunters had seen, and which had evidently been carefully kept during her eighteen years of solitude. Who shall tell why?
Two or three of the men

, extended in a line across the valley, were gradually approaching, and, watching these attentively, she did not at first perceive us. When at last we called her attention to ourselves, she at once arose, without any sign of fear or surprise, and making a curtsey, gave us a smiling welcome as she beckoned us into the camp. In speaking with her we found that while she apparently understood all we said, her speech was to us unintelligible.
had assembled and we asked her to go down to

, she hesitated with a look of perplexity; then, going outside, she began to pick the flowers. Having gathered a large bunch, she walked about fifty yards away and knelt down. We followed her movements curiously. Unable to see what she was doing, we drew nearer, and then saw she had spread the flowers over a little mound surmounted by a rude cross. In a few minutes she was ready to go.

"Arrived here, the whole people, Spanish and American, those who had known her in the old days as well as those who had come since, seemed to vie with each other to make pleasant the rest of her life. But there was little that could be done. Her wants were few, and her mind remained darkened, only gentleness and a desire to do little kindnesses showing itself like an instinct. She was never able to give a clear account of her life upon
. In a few months she sickened and died, and attended by the whole population in funeral procession, she was laid to rest by the side of
, who loved her so well, and had sorrowed for her so grievously.
, in conclusion, and we all reverently echoed "Amen."
